card.title: Neuroplasticity: Your Brain Rewires Itself
card.teaser: Your brain at 60 is not the brain you were born with — and not just because it's older. It's been physically rewiring all along.
card.coverImagePrompt: A glowing network of branching neurons rearranging themselves over time, time-lapse style with new connections sparking into existence, cinematic macro

lessons.title: An old idea that turned out to be wrong
lessons.body: For most of the 20th century, textbooks insisted the adult brain was fixed. Whatever you were born with, or developed as a child, was what you got. Then patients started recovering from strokes that should have been permanent. Blind people started 'seeing' through their tongues. The textbooks were wrong.

visual.imagePrompt: An old medical textbook being dramatically rewritten by hand with fresh ink, close-up, moody lighting

lessons.title: What 'plasticity' actually means
lessons.body: Neuroplasticity is the brain's ability to physically change in response to experience. That change happens at two scales. Structural plasticity: neurons grow new dendrites, form new synapses, and sometimes relocate entire functions from one brain region to another. Functional plasticity: existing connections get strengthened or weakened based on how often they fire. The brain isn't software on fixed hardware. The hardware itself is the software.

keyTerms.definition: The junction where one neuron passes a signal to another
The strengthening of a synapse through repeated firing — the cellular basis of learning

visual.imagePrompt: Two neurons with a glowing synapse between them, sparks visible across the gap

lessons.title: Neurons that fire together, wire together
lessons.body: The one-sentence summary comes from Donald Hebb in 1949: cells that repeatedly activate at the same time grow stronger connections. Practice a piano scale a thousand times and the neurons for those finger movements literally thicken their links. Stop practicing, and unused pathways get pruned — the brain is ruthless about real estate. This is why skills feel effortful at first and automatic later: they've moved from slow deliberate circuits to fast dedicated ones.

visual.imagePrompt: A pianist's hands in motion with faintly glowing nerve pathways extending from fingers up the arm

lessons.title: London cabbies and bigger brains
lessons.body: A famous 2000 study scanned the brains of London taxi drivers, who must memorize 25,000 streets to pass 'The Knowledge' exam. Their posterior hippocampi — regions involved in spatial memory — were measurably larger than controls', and bigger still in drivers with more years on the job. Stroke rehabilitation tells a similar story: forcing patients to use a weakened limb can coax neighboring brain areas to take over lost function, sometimes recovering movement doctors once called impossible.
lessons.funFact: When retired, cabbies' hippocampi start shrinking back toward normal size. The brain rents its real estate, it doesn't sell it.

visual.imagePrompt: A London black cab driving through foggy streets at night with an overlaid brain scan highlighting the hippocampus

lessons.title: You are under construction
lessons.body: Plasticity doesn't mean you can rewire yourself into anything — genes, age, and injury set real limits. But it does mean the brain you'll have tomorrow is partly a consequence of what you do today. Every practiced skill, every repeated thought pattern, leaves a fingerprint in physical tissue. That's a responsibility as much as a freedom.

quiz.question: Which principle summarizes Hebbian learning?
options: Neurons that fire together, wire together
quiz.explanation: Donald Hebb proposed that repeated co-activation strengthens the connection between neurons — the foundation of learning.

quiz.question: What did the London taxi driver study reveal?
options: Cabbies have smaller hippocampi from stress
Their posterior hippocampi were enlarged, more so with experience
The adult brain cannot change in size
quiz.explanation: Memorizing London's street network was associated with physical enlargement of spatial-memory regions.

quiz.question: What happens to unused neural pathways over time?
options: They get pruned back through lack of reinforcement
They migrate to other brain regions
quiz.explanation: The brain prunes connections that aren't being used — a hallmark of how plasticity conserves resources.

sources.title: Navigation-related structural change in the hippocampi of taxi drivers
sources.publisher: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
